subject: ent-written
title: ENT Written — Exam Notes
description: High-yield written-exam notes — ear, hearing/vestibular, airway/larynx, nose/sinuses, oral cavity, salivary glands, neck — plus an oral-topics recap.

sections.title: Ear — beyond the orals
sections.html:
Auricular trauma & infections (heavily tested)

ConditionKey fact
Othematoma / auricular haematomaCollection of blood between cartilage and perichondrium on the anterior surface of the auricle. Trauma. Must drain → otherwise "cauliflower ear". Acquired, not congenital.
PerichondritisInfection of cartilage/perichondrium — acquired. Pseudomonas common.
Erysipelas of the auricleCaused by Streptococcus.
Microtia, preauricular fistula, preauricular tagAll congenital.
"Which auricular condition is acquired, not congenital?" → othematoma / perichondritis. "Othematoma is…" → blood between cartilage & perichondrium, anterior surface.

Otosclerosis

Otospongiosis fixing the stapes footplate → progressive conductive hearing loss (later mixed). Affects women more, accelerated by pregnancy. Paracusis Willisii, Carhart's notch (2 kHz), Schwartze sign. Treated surgically: stapedectomy / stapedotomy.

The false statement in past papers: "main symptoms are SNHL, tinnitus, vertigo" — wrong, otosclerosis causes conductive loss.

Secretory otitis media (OME / glue ear)

Due to Eustachian tube dysfunction. Affects ~30% of children 3–6 yr. Type B (flat) tympanogram (NOT type A). Causes conductive loss → #1 cause of conductive HL in a child 3–6. In an adult with unilateral OME → exclude nasopharyngeal carcinoma.

"Child 3–6 with conductive HL, most likely Dx?" → secretory otitis media. "NOT true about secretory OM" → "tympanogram type A" (it's type B).

Sound transduction (physiology)

Conversion of mechanical → electrical energy happens at the hair cells of the cochlea (organ of Corti) — not the ossicles or the nerve.

Acoustic trauma & labyrinthine concussion

Acute acoustic trauma (blast, firearm, firecracker): tinnitus + SNHL, 4 kHz notch. Does NOT present with bleeding from the ear canal.

Labyrinthine concussion: impact of the membranous labyrinth against the bony walls of its capsule (no fracture needed). Causes vertigo/SNHL.

Cholesteatoma — formation

Forms by retraction + perforation in the epitympanic space (attic / pars flaccida) of the TM. Keratinising squamous epithelium erodes bone ("unsafe" ear).

Tympanic membrane anatomy

Separates external from middle ear. Two parts: pars tensa + pars flaccida. On otoscopy the handle/lateral process of the malleus is seen (not the stapes). Part of the middle, not inner, ear.

Otitis externa — treatment

Analgesics + topical antibiotic/steroid ear drops + aural toilet, keep dry. (Bug = Pseudomonas.) Not primarily oral antibiotics.

"AOM treatment does NOT include" → washing the external canal (that's for otitis externa). AOM = analgesia + antibiotics ± grommets + decongestants.

sections.title: SNHL, vertigo, nystagmus & facial nerve
sections.html:
Sensorineural hearing loss — management & congenital causes

  • Cannot be cured — aided with hearing aids if loss does not exceed ~80–90 dB; beyond that → cochlear implant.
  • Serious consequences on speech development when it occurs in childhood (→ screen newborns).
  • Congenital: CMV = leading non-genetic cause of childhood SNHL; rubella (+ cataract, cardiac); genetic (connexin-26); perinatal hypoxia/kernicterus.
  • Sudden SNHL = emergency → urgent steroids. Asymmetric/unilateral → MRI for acoustic neuroma.
"SNHL — which is true?" → aids if ≤80–90 dB + childhood speech impact (NOT "needs long-term drugs").

BPPV (written framing)

Short episodes (seconds) of rotational vertigo on lying down/getting up. Dx = head-repositioning / Dix-Hallpike test; treatment = specific therapeutic manoeuvres (Epley), NOT drugs. Not only the elderly.

Labyrinthitis vs vestibular neuritis

Labyrinthitis confirmed by rotational vertigo + sensorineural hearing loss (± tinnitus, nystagmus). Vestibular neuritis = vertigo WITHOUT hearing loss.

Nystagmus interpretation

Rhythmic eye movement (slow + fast phase). Vertical nystagmus → central (CNS) lesion. Peripheral = horizontal/rotatory, suppressed by fixation. Occurs in both irritative and destructive vestibular lesions.

Facial nerve — Bell's palsy & Frey/otocranial syndrome

Bell's palsy treatment = corticosteroids (± antivirals); LMN palsy involving the forehead.

Frey's syndrome (gustatory sweating / "otocranial-temporomandibular" syndrome) = aberrant parasympathetic reinnervation after parotidectomy → sweating/flushing over the cheek when eating.

"Drug for facial nerve paralysis" → corticosteroids. "Syndrome after parotidectomy" → Frey's.

sections.title: Airway, tracheostomy, laryngeal cancer & voice
sections.html:
Emergency airway management

  • 1st choice = endotracheal intubation.
  • Coniotomy / cricothyroidotomy = emergency, TEMPORARY airway (2–3 min) — not permanent.
  • Tracheostomy = can be temporary or permanent; conservative measures = O₂ + nebulised adrenaline.

Tracheostomy — indications & types

Indications: (1) bypass an obstruction above the trachea, (2) airway/pulmonary toilet (care of tracheobronchial tree), (3) prolonged intubation (avoid laryngeal/tracheal stenosis), (4) prophylactic in major head-neck surgery, (5) percutaneous failure.

Types: planned · emergency (5–10 min) · extremely emergency (2–3 min) · minitrach; high/mid/low; percutaneous vs open.

"Indications for tracheotomy" → bypass obstruction above trachea + avoid stenosis in prolonged intubation ("both a and c").

Laryngeal cancer — prognosis & etiology

Subsite5-yr survivalNote
Glottic79% (best)Smoking; early hoarseness; sparse lymphatics
Supraglottic47%Smoking + alcohol; rich lymphatics → early bilateral nodes
Subglottic30% (worst)Rare, presents late

SCC is the commonest. T1 → radiotherapy ≈ laser cordectomy (>90% cure). Nodal metastases markedly worsen survival.

Common trap: "glottic carcinoma has a worse prognosis" is FALSE — glottic has the best prognosis.

Voice rehabilitation after total laryngectomy

Three options: (1) oesophageal speech, (2) tracheo-oesophageal voice prosthesis, (3) electrolarynx (handheld). Speech therapy has a role.

Laryngeal nerve palsies

NerveEffect
Superior laryngeal n. (external br.)Paralysis of cricothyroid → altered vocal range, difficulty singing/high pitch
Unilateral RLNHoarseness (cord paramedian)
Bilateral RLN (e.g. after total thyroidectomy)Cords paramedian → inspiratory stridor / airway obstruction — most significant symptom

Vocal cord paralysis causes: thyroid/parathyroid surgery, idiopathic, cervical spine surgery, lung/oesophageal/mediastinal tumour, aortic aneurysm. NOT lower-limb surgery.

Benign voice disorders

  • Vocal cord nodules: bilateral, "singer's nodules", from voice abuse → conservative / voice therapy (not primarily surgery). Don't cause dyspnoea, not malignant.
  • Vocal cord granuloma: posterior third, from trauma/intubation/reflux; treat with anti-reflux + avoid trauma, surgery if persistent; recurs; not precancerous.
  • Functional / mutational dysphonia: occurs at puberty (persistent high-pitched voice in boys); hyperfunctional dysphonia → voice therapy, not surgery.

Subglottic — narrowest in children; stenosis; acute laryngitis

In children the subglottis is the narrowest point → oedema most dangerous there. Subglottic stenosis: commonly from prolonged intubation; dyspnoea + inspiratory stridor; MRI has NO role (use endoscopy/CT). Larynx skeleton = cartilages (3 unpaired: thyroid, cricoid, epiglottis + 3 paired: arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform) with the hyoid bone above.

sections.title: Nose, sinuses, smell & epistaxis (written detail)
sections.html:
Nasal anatomy facts that get tested

  • Nasal septum = quadrilateral (septal) cartilage + vomer + perpendicular plate of the ethmoid.
  • Nasal valve = angle (10–15°) between upper lateral cartilage and septum; narrowest part of the airway; regulates airflow. (The nasal cavity "contains the nasal valve".)
  • Nasal cycle = cyclical alternation of patency every 2–5 hours.
  • External nose blood supply: facial → angular (ECA); dorsal nasal (ophthalmic, ICA); infraorbital (maxillary). Angular vein → cavernous sinus (danger area).
  • Olfactory epithelium: roof / superior concha + upper septum; filaments pierce the cribriform plate.

Smell disorders

Anosmia / hyposmia / parosmia / phantosmia. Conductive (airflow blocked: polyps, rhinitis, septal deviation) vs sensorineural (olfactory neuroepithelium/nerve). Common causes: sinonasal disease (#1), post-viral (incl. COVID), head trauma (shearing at cribriform plate), ageing, congenital (Kallmann = anosmia + hypogonadism).

Congenital nasal obstruction

Causes: choanal atresia, nasal glioma, meningoencephalocele ("all of the above").

Choanal atresia: 90% bony, 10% membranous; bilateral = neonatal emergency (cyclical cyanosis relieved by crying); associated with CHARGE syndrome.

CSF rhinorrhoea

Clear watery nasal discharge (vs malignancy, FB, bacterial rhinitis which are not watery). Confirm with beta-2 transferrin; halo/ring sign. Often post-trauma/surgery.

Acute rhinosinusitis — site, complications, imaging

  • Most often: ethmoid in children, maxillary in adults.
  • Complications: ophthalmic + intracranial + osseous ("all of the above"). Orbital is commonest (ethmoid). Orbital cellulitis / abscess needs surgery. Osseous = Pott's puffy tumour (frontal).
  • Imaging: CT has largely replaced plain X-ray; CT for bone/ostia, MRI for soft tissue/intracranial. Ultrasound/MRI are NOT first choice.

Nasal polyps (written points)

Treated with corticosteroid sprays + endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS); frequently recur; associated with asthma, allergic rhinitis, aspirin intolerance (Samter), and in children → cystic fibrosis. NOT a cause of severe epistaxis (that's juvenile angiofibroma). Originate around the middle meatus, not the floor; benign.

Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma ("nasal fibroma of young age")

Benign but locally aggressive, very vascular; adolescent boys; presents with severe recurrent epistaxis + nasal obstruction. Biopsy contraindicated (bleeding); treat surgery ± embolisation. Not treated with radio/chemo first-line; not malignant.

Epistaxis & nasal fractures (written framing)

Commonest site = Little's area (anterior septum), NOT the middle concha. Minor childhood bleeds → silver-nitrate cautery. Anterior-posterior packing → hospitalise; packing >48 h → antibiotics. NOT a cause: vasomotor rhinitis (causes = vWD, Osler/HHT, anticoagulants, cardiac).

Nasal fractures: crepitus, displacement, epistaxis; do NOT always need immediate surgery (reduce within ~2 weeks). Drain a septal haematoma urgently.

sections.title: Oral cavity & oropharynx (big written topic)
sections.html:
Trismus — definition & causes

Trismus = difficulty/inability opening the mouth. Acute painful causes: peritonsillar abscess, parapharyngeal abscess, Ludwig's angina, alveolar (last molar) infection, acute parotitis, TMJ arthritis, condylar fracture.

Precancerous & malignant oral lesions

  • Precancerous: leukoplakia + erythroplakia (erythroplakia higher risk). Lichen planus = potentially malignant too.
  • Oral SCC: squamous epithelium; smoking + alcohol + HPV + poor hygiene + sun; males; exophytic/ulcerative; 40–50% have nodal mets (submental/submandibular/cervical). Persistent ulcer >2 weeks → biopsy.
  • Lower lip cancer: sun exposure + pipe smoking + age.
  • Commonest intra-oral sites: floor of mouth + lateral/ventral tongue.

Ranula & mucocele

Ranula = saliva-retention cyst in the floor of mouth (sublingual gland); bluish, translucent; treat by marsupialisation/excision (not just aspiration). Mucocele = minor salivary gland retention cyst, often lower lip.

Infections & mucosal conditions

  • Herpetic gingivostomatitis = HSV type 1.
  • Aphthous ulcers (recurrent painful), Vincent's angina (fusospirochaetal), candidiasis, angular cheilitis, HIV hairy leukoplakia (EBV, lateral tongue, not premalignant), Kaposi's sarcoma.
  • Ludwig's angina: bilateral cellulitis of submandibular/sublingual/submental spaces, odontogenic, floor of mouth swelling, tongue elevated, airway emergency.

Tongue conditions

Macroglossia → Down syndrome, acromegaly (also amyloid, hypothyroidism). Black hairy tongue = elongated filiform papillae. Geographic tongue (lingua geographica), fissured tongue, ankyloglossia (tongue-tie), lingual thyroid, Hunter's glossitis (B12).

Tongue innervation (written wording)

  • Taste, anterior 2/3 → chorda tympani (VII).
  • Motor (all muscles) → hypoglossal (XII) (except palatoglossus = X).
  • General sensation ant 2/3 = lingual (V3); post 1/3 sensation+taste = IX.

Oropharyngeal carcinoma

Tonsil / base of tongue. Etiology: alcohol + tobacco + HPV (HPV, NOT EBV — EBV is nasopharynx). Often asymptomatic early; foreign-body sensation, dysphagia, referred otalgia; frequently presents with cervical nodal metastasis. Treated surgery / RT / chemo / combinations.

"Oropharyngeal cancer is related to … EBV" = the FALSE statement. It's HPV.

Zenker's diverticulum & OSA work-up

Zenker's diverticulum = pharyngeal (hypopharyngeal) pouch through Killian's dehiscence → dysphagia, regurgitation, halitosis. Located in the pharynx.

OSA investigation: oropharyngoscopy, flexible nasopharyngolaryngoscopy + Mueller's manoeuvre, sleep study (polysomnography). NOT brain CT.

Palatine tonsil blood supply

From branches of the external carotid artery (tonsillar branch of facial a., + ascending pharyngeal, lingual, lesser palatine).

sections.title: Salivary glands (full written detail)
sections.html:
Duct openings & saliva

GlandDuct → opensSaliva
ParotidStensen → opposite 2nd upper molar; serous25%
SubmandibularWharton → sublingual caruncle (floor of mouth, beside frenulum); mixed70%
SublingualRivinus/Bartholin → floor of mouth; mucous5%

Total saliva 500–1500 mL/day. Functions: cleansing, lubrication, antimicrobial, taste dissolution, starch digestion (amylase), bolus formation.

Sialolithiasis

80–90% in Wharton's duct (submandibular) — duct runs uphill, viscous Ca/PO₄-rich secretion. Submandibular stones are often radiolucent (80%) → US/CT/sialography. Mealtime swelling + pain. Tx: hydration/milking, sialendoscopy, duct incision, lithotripsy, gland excision.

"Sudden submandibular swelling, painful, worse with meals, then subsides" → sialolithiasis.

Inflammatory / infective

  • Mumps: usually bilateral, non-purulent saliva, low fever; can cause SNHL + orchitis/pancreatitis.
  • Acute suppurative sialadenitis: Staph aureus, dehydrated/elderly, purulent.
  • Recurrent/chronic sialadenitis, TB, cat-scratch.

Sialosis (sialadenosis) & Sjögren's

Sialosis = persistent, bilateral, painless, non-inflammatory, non-neoplastic parotid swelling. Causes: alcoholism, bulimia/malnutrition, diabetes, hypothyroidism, cirrhosis, drugs.

Sjögren's: autoimmune → dry eyes + dry mouth + bilateral parotid swelling; anti-Ro/La; ↑↑ risk of MALT lymphoma.

Salivary tumours — the statistics

Gland% of tumoursMalignant risk
Parotid64–80%~20% malignant (80% benign)
Submandibular10–15%~50% malignant
Minor / sublingual~15%>50% malignant

Rule: the smaller the gland, the higher the malignant chance. Main treatment = surgery (not chemotherapy). In children, commonest salivary tumour = haemangioma (involutes).

"Main treatment for salivary gland tumours is chemotherapy" = FALSE → it's surgery.

Specific tumours

  • Pleomorphic adenoma — commonest, benign, painless; can recur; carcinoma-ex-pleomorphic if neglected.
  • Warthin's — benign, older male smokers, bilateral/multifocal, increased uptake on Tc-99 / PET.
  • Mucoepidermoid carcinoma — commonest malignant.
  • Adenoid cystic carcinoma — perineural spread → pain + VII palsy.
  • Acinic cell carcinoma.

Signs of malignancy & Frey's syndrome

Malignancy: facial-nerve palsy (most reliable), skin ulceration/fixation, rapid growth, sensory nerve paraesthesia, nodes, trismus, pain, minor-gland location.

Frey's syndrome = gustatory sweating after parotidectomy (auriculotemporal nerve misdirection).

sections.title: Neck masses, thyroid cancer & foreign bodies
sections.html:
Cervical swelling — diagnostic approach (very testable)

  • In adults, ~80% of neck masses are not benign-trivial; over 40 → never assume benign (think malignancy/mets).
  • Night sweats + >10% weight loss → suspect malignancy/lymphoma, investigate.
  • Supraclavicular nodes → lung/GI mets + lymphoma. Posterior triangle multiple small mobile nodes → TB or NPC mets.
  • Work-up order: FNA cytology, ultrasound, CT; open biopsy LAST (after full work-up). FNA evaluates positive findings (not just to exclude).
  • Under 20 → reactive/congenital more likely (not metastatic carcinoma).

Congenital neck masses

LesionKey
Thyroglossal cystAnywhere foramen cecum → sternal notch; midline; moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion
Dermoid cystMidline; moves with swallowing but NOT tongue protrusion
Branchial cyst2nd arch; lateral, anterior to upper SCM, young adult
Cystic hygromaInfant; soft, transilluminates, posterior triangle

Thyroid carcinoma — types & prognosis

TypeNote
PapillaryCommonest, best prognosis, lymphatic spread
FollicularHaematogenous (lung/bone)
MedullaryParafollicular C cells, calcitonin, MEN 2
AnaplasticWorst prognosis, elderly, rapidly fatal

Malignancy features: hard fixed solitary nodule, rapid growth, nodes, stridor, dysphagia, hoarseness (RLN invasion).

Foreign bodies — airway

  • Toddlers (oral exploration, immature laryngeal reflexes, no molars). Aspirate during sudden reaction with food in mouth.
  • Aspirated objects: vegetable matter 70–80% (peanuts/nuts ~35%); lodge in bronchi 80–90% (right main).
  • Often radiolucent; removal under GA via laryngo-tracheo-bronchoscopy.
  • Prevention: avoid nuts/small toys in under-3s + parent education.

Foreign bodies — oesophageal & nasal

  • Oesophageal: most common at the 1st (cricopharyngeal) narrowing, then aortic/bronchial, then GE junction. Disc battery = emergency (mucosal damage in 1 h, perforation 8–12 h) → urgent removal.
  • Nasal: children; unilateral foul discharge; after removal check for a second FB; remove only with proper light/tools.

sections.title: Oral-Covered Recap
sections.html:
These topics dominate the oral stations and are in your Oral Trainer. They also appear on the written paper, so here's a condensed refresher. For full depth + flashcards, use the Oral Trainer file.

🔁 Strategy: spend most new study time on the Written-Focus tab. Treat this tab as a 10-minute confidence pass over what you already drilled for the oral.

Epistaxis

Local vs general causes; Little's area (90%); cautery → packing → SPA ligation; Thudichum speculum.

Cervical lymph node levels

I–VII; Virchow = left supraclavicular; malignant = hard/fixed/>2cm/supraclavicular.

Sinus drainage

Inferior=nasolacrimal; middle=frontal/maxillary/ant+mid ethmoid; superior=post ethmoid; sphenoethmoidal=sphenoid.

Sphenoid relations

ICA, optic nerve, pituitary/sella, cavernous sinus, Vidian + maxillary nerves.

Otitis media

Pneumococcus/H.flu/Moraxella; complications intratemporal + intracranial; sigmoid (not cavernous) sinus thrombosis.

NPC

Fossa of Rosenmüller; EBV; neck node + unilateral adult OME + epistaxis + CN palsies.

Peritonsillar abscess

Unilateral; trismus, hot-potato voice, uvula deviated away; aspirate/I&D + antibiotics.

Referred otalgia

V, IX, X (larynx → supraglottic Ca), C2/3. Normal otoscopy + ear pain = hunt the source.

Vertigo

Peripheral vs central; Ménière's (4 features), BPPV (seconds), labyrinthitis (+SNHL).

Weber / Rinne

Weber→bad ear + Rinne negative = conductive; Weber→good ear + Rinne positive = SNHL.

Conductive vs SNHL causes

Conductive: wax, OME, perforation, otosclerosis, cholesteatoma. SNHL: presbycusis, noise, ototoxic, Ménière's, acoustic neuroma.

Ototoxic drugs

Aminoglycosides, cisplatin, loop diuretics, salicylates, quinine. NOT penicillins/cephalosporins.

Tonsillitis

GAS; complications: quinsy, rheumatic fever, glomerulonephritis; tonsillectomy indications.

Adenoid hypertrophy

Mouth breathing, hyponasal voice, OME, adenoid facies, OSA.

Allergic rhinitis

Sneeze/rhinorrhoea/itch/congestion; pale boggy turbinates; intranasal steroid + antihistamine.

Facial palsy

LMN (whole face) vs UMN (forehead spared); Bell's = steroids; Ramsay Hunt.

Temporal bone #

Longitudinal (conductive, otorrhoea) vs transverse (SNHL, vertigo, facial palsy).

Leukoplakia

White patch, can't rub off, premalignant → biopsy; erythroplakia worse.

Hoarseness / dysphagia / stridor

Extra-laryngeal hoarseness (RLN, hypothyroid, GERD); dysphagia + dyspnoea cause lists.

Salivary basics

Pleomorphic adenoma commonest; Warthin bilateral; VII palsy = malignancy; sialolithiasis.
